title: Path with minimum effort (grid + Dijkstra/binary search).
description: Path with minimum effort (grid + Dijkstra/binary search).

examples.explanation: Start at (0,0)=1, min_effort=∞. Path 1: (0,0)→(0,1): diff=|1-2|=1, max=1; (0,1)→(0,2): diff=|2-2|=0, max=1; (0,2)→(1,2): diff=|2-2|=0, max=1; (1,2)→(2,2): diff=|2-5|=3, max=3; reach end, min_effort=3. Backtrack to explore other paths. Path 2: (0,0)→(1,0): diff=|1-3|=2, max=2; (1,0)→(2,0): diff=|3-5|=2, max=2; (2,0)→(2,1): diff=|5-3|=2, max=2; (2,1)→(2,2): diff=|3-5|=2, max=2; reach end, min_effort=2. Continue exploring other paths but none yield <2. Final min_effort=2.

Start at (0,0)=1. Explore paths systematically. One optimal path: (0,0)→(0,1): diff=|1-2|=1, max=1; (0,1)→(0,2): diff=|2-3|=1, max=1; (0,2)→(1,2): diff=|3-4|=1, max=1; (1,2)→(2,2): diff=|4-5|=1, max=1; reach end, min_effort=1. Other paths have higher max differences (e.g., through 8 gives diff=7). Final min_effort=1.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(4^(m*n))
approaches.summary: Explore all possible paths from start to end using depth-first search, tracking the maximum absolute difference in heights along each path, and return the minimum of these maximums. This is straightforward but extremely slow for larger grids due to exponential growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DFS for Minimum Effort Path
ladders.desc: Find the minimum possible maximum absolute height difference along any path from top-left to bottom-right in a grid by exploring all possible paths using DFS.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding a path from the top-left cell (0,0) to the bottom-right cell (m-1,n-1) in an m×n grid where each cell has an integer height. The 'effort' of a path is defined as the maximum absolute difference in heights between consecutive cells along that path. Our goal is to find the minimum possible effort among all valid paths. The brute force DFS approach systematically explores every possible path from start to end using depth-first search. For each path, we track the maximum height difference encountered so far. When we reach the destination, we update a global minimum effort variable if the current path's maximum difference is smaller. The intuition is that by exploring all possible paths, we're guaranteed to find the optimal solution, but this comes at the cost of exponential time complexity. The algorithm works because it's essentially performing an exhaustive search through the state space of all possible paths. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize min_effort to infinity. 2. Create a visited matrix to track cells in current path. 3. Define DFS function that takes current position and current_max_diff. 4. If at destination, update min_effort with min(min_effort, current_max_diff). 5. Otherwise, for each of the 4 possible directions (up, down, left, right), if the neighbor is within bounds and not visited, calculate the height difference between current and neighbor, update current_max_diff to max(current_max_diff, diff), mark neighbor visited, recurse, then unmark visited (backtracking). 6. Start DFS from (0,0) with current_max_diff = 0. Time complexity is O(4^(m*n)) in worst case because at each cell we have up to 4 choices and we might explore all possible paths. Space complexity is O(m*n) for the recursion stack and visited matrix. Connection to L1: This full solution relies on the DFS traversal with backtracking as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include: not handling the base case properly (updating min_effort only at destination), forgetting to backtrack (which would prevent exploring all paths), and incorrectly calculating the maximum difference (should be max of current_max_diff and new difference, not sum). Real code pattern: def dfs(i, j, current_max): if i==m-1 and j==n-1: min_effort = min(min_effort, current_max); return; for each direction: calculate diff = abs(heights[i][j]-heights[ni][nj]); new_max = max(current_max, diff); mark visited; dfs(ni, nj, new_max); unmark visited.

examples.trace: Start at (0,0)=1, min_effort=∞. Path 1: (0,0)→(0,1): diff=|1-2|=1, max=1; (0,1)→(0,2): diff=|2-2|=0, max=1; (0,2)→(1,2): diff=|2-2|=0, max=1; (1,2)→(2,2): diff=|2-5|=3, max=3; reach end, min_effort=3. Backtrack to explore other paths. Path 2: (0,0)→(1,0): diff=|1-3|=2, max=2; (1,0)→(2,0): diff=|3-5|=2, max=2; (2,0)→(2,1): diff=|5-3|=2, max=2; (2,1)→(2,2): diff=|3-5|=2, max=2; reach end, min_effort=2. Continue exploring other paths but none yield <2. Final min_effort=2.

Start at (0,0)=1. Explore paths systematically. One optimal path: (0,0)→(0,1): diff=|1-2|=1, max=1; (0,1)→(0,2): diff=|2-3|=1, max=1; (0,2)→(1,2): diff=|3-4|=1, max=1; (1,2)→(2,2): diff=|4-5|=1, max=1; reach end, min_effort=1. Other paths have higher max differences (e.g., through 8 gives diff=7). Final min_effort=1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal with Backtracking for Grid Paths
ladders.desc: Implement depth-first search that explores all possible paths in a grid while tracking visited cells and backtracking to explore alternatives.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach: a DFS that systematically explores all paths from a starting cell to a destination cell in a grid. The key insight is using backtracking—marking cells as visited when entering a path branch, then unmarking them when leaving that branch—to ensure all possible paths are explored. Without backtracking, we would only explore one path and miss alternatives. The intuition is that we're building a search tree where each node represents a cell, and edges represent moves to neighbors. By recursively exploring each possible direction and then undoing the visit mark, we ensure the entire tree is traversed. Step-by-step: 1. Define a recursive function dfs(i, j) that takes current coordinates. 2. If (i,j) is the destination, process the result (in full problem, update min effort). 3. Otherwise, mark (i,j) as visited. 4. For each of the 4 directions (up, down, left, right): if neighbor is within bounds and not visited, recursively call dfs on neighbor. 5. After exploring all directions from (i,j), unmark (i,j) as visited (backtracking). Time complexity for this sub-routine alone is O(4^(m*n)) in worst case because each cell can lead to 4 recursive calls. Space complexity is O(m*n) for recursion stack and visited matrix. Connection to L0: This DFS with backtracking is exactly what's used in the full solution to explore all paths. Connection to L2: The backtracking mechanism enables the exhaustive search. Common mistakes: forgetting to unmark visited cells (leads to incomplete exploration), not checking bounds before accessing neighbors (causes index errors), and infinite recursion if destination check is missing. Real code pattern: visited = [[False]*n for _ in range(m)]; def dfs(i,j): if i==dest_i and j==dest_j: # process; return; visited[i][j]=True; for di,dj in directions: ni,nj=i+di,j+dj; if 0<=ni<m and 0<=nj<n and not visited[ni][nj]: dfs(ni,nj); visited[i][j]=False.

examples.input: Grid 2x2, start=(0,0), end=(1,1), find all paths
examples.trace: Start dfs(0,0): mark visited. From (0,0), two neighbors: (0,1) and (1,0). First explore (0,1): dfs(0,1): mark visited. From (0,1), neighbors: (0,0) visited, (1,1) not visited. dfs(1,1): destination reached, record path. Backtrack: unmark (1,1), return to (0,1), unmark (0,1). Back to (0,0). Now explore (1,0): dfs(1,0): mark visited. From (1,0), neighbors: (0,0) visited, (1,1) not visited. dfs(1,1): destination reached, record path. Backtrack: unmark (1,1), return to (1,0), unmark (1,0). Back to (0,0), unmark (0,0). Done.

examples.input: Grid 1x3, start=(0,0), end=(0,2), find all paths
examples.trace: Start dfs(0,0): mark visited. Only neighbor is (0,1) (left/right only in 1D). dfs(0,1): mark visited. From (0,1), neighbor (0,2) not visited. dfs(0,2): destination reached, record path. Backtrack: unmark (0,2), return to (0,1), unmark (0,1), return to (0,0), unmark (0,0). Only one path exists.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Tracking Maximum Difference Along a Path
ladders.desc: While traversing a path, maintain the maximum absolute height difference between consecutive cells encountered so far.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique in the minimum effort problem: as we traverse a path, we need to track the 'current maximum difference'—the largest absolute difference in heights between any two consecutive cells along the path from start to current position. The intuition is that the effort of a path is defined as this maximum difference, so to evaluate a path, we must compute this value incrementally. At each step moving from cell A to cell B, we calculate diff = |height(A)-height(B)|, then update current_max = max(current_max, diff). This ensures current_max always holds the maximum difference seen so far on this path. This is efficient because it's O(1) per move and avoids storing all differences. The algorithm: 1. Initialize current_max = 0 at start cell (no movement yet). 2. For each move from cell (i,j) to neighbor (ni,nj): diff = abs(heights[i][j] - heights[ni][nj]); current_max = max(current_max, diff). 3. Pass current_max recursively to next steps. Time complexity per path is O(L) where L is path length. Space is O(1) extra. Connection to L1: In the DFS, we pass current_max as a parameter to the recursive function. Connection to L3: This relies on calculating absolute differences between grid cells. Common mistakes: resetting current_max incorrectly (should persist along path), using sum instead of max, or not initializing to 0. Real code pattern: In dfs(i,j,current_max): for move to neighbor: new_diff = abs(heights[i][j]-heights[ni][nj]); new_max = max(current_max, new_diff); dfs(ni,nj,new_max).

examples.input: Path: cells with heights [1,3,5,2], track max diff
examples.trace: Start at height 1, current_max=0. Move to 3: diff=|1-3|=2, current_max=max(0,2)=2. Move to 5: diff=|3-5|=2, current_max=max(2,2)=2. Move to 2: diff=|5-2|=3, current_max=max(2,3)=3. Final current_max=3.
Start at 4, current_max=0. Move to 4: diff=|4-4|=0, current_max=max(0,0)=0. Move to 4: diff=0, current_max=0. Move to 4: diff=0, current_max=0. Final current_max=0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Calculating Absolute Differences in a Grid
ladders.desc: Compute the absolute difference between heights of two adjacent cells in a grid (up, down, left, right).
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves accessing grid values and computing absolute differences between neighboring cells. In a 2D grid represented as a list of lists, each cell (i,j) has up to 4 neighbors: (i-1,j), (i+1,j), (i,j-1), (i,j+1), provided they are within grid bounds. The absolute difference between two cells is |height1 - height2|, which is always non-negative and represents the effort needed to move between them. The intuition is that this difference quantifies the 'cost' of a move in the path effort problem. Step-by-step: 1. Given grid heights[][] with dimensions m×n. 2. For a move from (i,j) to (ni,nj): first ensure both coordinates are valid (0≤i,ni<m and 0≤j,nj<n). 3. Retrieve values: a = heights[i][j]; b = heights[ni][nj]. 4. Compute diff = |a - b| using absolute value function. Time complexity is O(1) per calculation. Space is O(1). Connection to L2: This difference calculation is used to update the current maximum along a path. Connection to L4: This relies on accessing grid elements by indices. Common mistakes: accessing out-of-bounds indices (cause runtime error), incorrect absolute value calculation (e.g., using negative values), or misunderstanding grid indexing (rows vs columns). Real code pattern: def get_diff(grid, i, j, ni, nj): if 0<=ni<len(grid) and 0<=nj<len(grid[0]): return abs(grid[i][j] - grid[ni][nj]); else: return None (or handle error).

examples.trace: Heights: at (0,0)=1, at (0,1)=2. Compute |1-2| = 1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Comparing Grid Elements
ladders.desc: Retrieve values from a 2D grid using row and column indices, and compare two integers.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves working with a 2D grid (matrix) represented as a list of lists in programming. Each inner list represents a row, and elements are accessed using two indices: grid[i][j] gives the value at row i, column j. Row indices typically start at 0 for the top row, and column indices at 0 for the leftmost column. Comparing two integers means using relational operators (<, >, <=, >=, ==, !=) or calculating their difference. The intuition is that grids are a common data structure for problems involving spatial relationships, and accessing elements is basic to any grid algorithm. Step-by-step: 1. Define grid as list of lists, e.g., grid = [[1,2],[3,4]]. 2. Access element at (i,j): value = grid[i][j]. Ensure i and j are within bounds: 0 ≤ i < number of rows, 0 ≤ j < number of columns. 3. Compare two values a and b: e.g., a < b returns True if a is less than b. Time and space are O(1). Connection to L3: This is used to get heights for difference calculation. Connection to L5: This builds on array indexing concepts. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in indices, confusing rows and columns (grid[i][j] vs grid[j][i]), or accessing empty lists. Real code pattern: rows = len(grid); cols = len(grid[0]); if 0<=i<rows and 0<=j<cols: val = grid[i][j]; else: handle error. Comparison: diff = a - b; or if a > b: ...

examples.trace: Grid has 2 rows, 3 columns. Row indices: 0 for [5,10,15], 1 for [20,25,30]. Column indices: 0,1,2. Access grid[1][2]: row 1, column 2 -> value 30.
Access grid[0][0]=1, grid[1][1]=4. Compare: 1 < 4 evaluates to True.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand how to store and access elements in a list (array) using indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: an array (or list in Python) is a data structure that stores a collection of elements in a linear order. Each element has an index (position) starting from 0 for the first element, 1 for the second, and so on. Accessing an element means retrieving its value using its index, e.g., arr[0] gives the first element. The intuition is that arrays allow efficient random access—you can get any element directly if you know its index, in O(1) time. This is fundamental because grids are essentially 2D arrays (lists of lists). Step-by-step: 1. Create a list: arr = [10, 20, 30]. 2. Access element at index i: value = arr[i]. For example, arr[1] returns 20. 3. Indices must be valid: 0 ≤ i < len(arr), where len(arr) gives the number of elements. If index is out of range (e.g., arr[5] for a 3-element list), it causes an error. Time complexity for access is O(1). Space is O(n) for storing n elements. Connection to L4: 2D grid access extends this to two indices. Common mistakes: using index 1 to get first element (should be 0), forgetting that indices start at 0, or accessing beyond the last index (off-by-one). Real code pattern: arr = [5, 10, 15]; first = arr[0]; # 5; second = arr[1]; # 10; length = len(arr); # 3.

examples.trace: Array has elements: index 0:3, index 1:7, index 2:1, index 3:9. Access arr[2] returns the element at position 2, which is 1.
Indices: 0->100, 1->200, 2->300. arr[0] returns the first element, 100.

approaches.name: Binary Search with BFS O(m*n*log(H))
approaches.summary: Use binary search on the possible effort values (from 0 to the maximum height difference in the grid) to find the minimum feasible effort. For each mid-value, perform BFS to check if a path exists where all adjacent differences are ≤ mid. This leverages monotonicity in feasibility.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(m*n*log(H)) where H is the range of heights

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Effort Path with Binary Search + BFS
ladders.desc: Find the minimum effort path in a height grid using binary search on effort values with BFS feasibility checks.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires finding a path from top-left to bottom-right in an m×n grid where each cell has a height. The effort of a path is defined as the maximum absolute difference in heights between any two consecutive cells along the path. We need to minimize this maximum effort. The binary search approach works because feasibility is monotonic: if a path exists with effort ≤ k, then a path also exists for any effort > k. We binary search over possible effort values from 0 to the maximum height difference in the grid. For each mid-value, we perform BFS to check if we can traverse from (0,0) to (m-1,n-1) while ensuring all adjacent cell differences are ≤ mid. If feasible, we try smaller efforts; otherwise, we try larger efforts. This yields O(m*n*log(H)) time where H is the height range, and O(m*n) space for BFS queue and visited set. The intuition leverages that we're searching for the minimum threshold where the grid becomes 'connected' under the effort constraint. Common mistakes include incorrect binary search bounds (should be 0 to max_height-min_height), not using visited sets in BFS (causing infinite loops), and mishandling edge cases like 1×1 grids. The connection to L1 is that this level integrates the binary search framework with the BFS feasibility check from L1.

examples.trace:
1. Grid: [[1,2,2],[3,8,2],[5,3,5]]. Min height=1, max height=8, so H=7. Binary search range [0,7].
2. Mid=3: BFS checks if path exists with effort ≤3. Start at (0,0)=1. Adjacent (0,1)=2 diff=1≤3, (1,0)=3 diff=2≤3. Continue BFS: reach (2,2)=5 from (2,1)=3 diff=2≤3. Path found: (0,0)→(0,1)→(0,2)→(1,2)→(2,2). Feasible.
3. Update high=3, mid=1. BFS with effort ≤1: From (0,0)=1, (0,1)=2 diff=1≤1 ok, but (1,1)=8 from (0,1) diff=6>1, and other paths fail. Not feasible.
4. Update low=2, mid=2. BFS with effort ≤2: Similar to step 2 but with tighter constraint. Path (0,0)→(1,0)→(2,0)→(2,1)→(2,2): diffs: |1-3|=2, |3-5|=2, |5-3|=2, |3-5|=2 all ≤2. Feasible.
5. Binary search ends with low=2. Return 2.

1. Grid: [[1,2,3],[3,8,4],[5,3,5]]. Min=1, max=8, H=7. Range [0,7].
2. Mid=3: BFS with effort ≤3. Start (0,0)=1. Explore: reach (2,2)=5 via path with max diff 2 (e.g., |3-5|=2). Feasible.
3. High=3, mid=1. BFS with effort ≤1: From (0,0)=1, (0,1)=2 diff=1≤1 ok, but (1,1)=8 diff=6>1 blocks. Try path (0,0)→(1,0)→(2,0)→(2,1)→(2,2): |1-3|=2>1 fails. Not feasible.
4. Low=2, mid=2. BFS with effort ≤2: Path (0,0)→(0,1)→(0,2)→(1,2)→(2,2): diffs: |1-2|=1, |2-3|=1, |3-4|=1, |4-5|=1 all ≤2. Feasible.
5. High=2, mid=1 (since low=2, high=2, mid=1? Wait, binary search: after step 3, low=2, high=3, mid=2. After step 4 feasible, set high=2. Now low=2, high=2, exit loop. Return low=2? Let's recalc: Start low=0, high=7. Mid=3 feasible → high=3. Mid=1 not feasible → low=2. Now low=2, high=3, mid=2 feasible → high=2. Now low=2, high=2, exit. Return 2. But expected output is 1? Check grid: path (0,0)→(0,1)→(0,2)→(1,2)→(2,2) has diffs: 1,1,1,1 max=1. So effort 1 should be feasible. BFS with effort=1: From (0,0)=1, go to (0,1)=2 diff=1 ok, to (0,2)=3 diff=1 ok, to (1,2)=4 diff=1 ok, to (2,2)=5 diff=1 ok. Yes feasible! So binary search: after mid=1 not feasible? I made error: In step 3, BFS with effort=1: from (0,0)=1, (0,1)=2 diff=1 ok, (0,2)=3 from (0,1) diff=1 ok, (1,2)=4 from (0,2) diff=1 ok, (2,2)=5 from (1,2) diff=1 ok. So it is feasible! Correct trace: 1. Range [0,7], mid=3 feasible → high=3. 2. Mid=1 feasible → high=1. 3. Mid=0 not feasible → low=1. 4. Low=1, high=1, return 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Feasibility Check for Given Effort
ladders.desc: Check if a path exists from start to end in a grid with all adjacent height differences ≤ a given effort using BFS.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine determines whether it's possible to traverse from the top-left cell (0,0) to the bottom-right cell (m-1,n-1) in an m×n height grid, given a maximum allowed effort k. The effort for moving between two adjacent cells (up, down, left, right) is the absolute difference in their heights. A path is feasible if all such efforts along the path are ≤ k. We use BFS (or DFS) to explore the grid starting from (0,0), only moving to neighboring cells if the height difference is ≤ k. We maintain a visited set to avoid cycles. If we reach (m-1,n-1), return True; otherwise, False after exploring all reachable cells. The intuition is that BFS systematically checks connectivity under the effort constraint, treating cells as nodes and edges as valid moves. Time complexity is O(m*n) since each cell is visited at most once, and space is O(m*n) for the queue and visited set. Common mistakes include forgetting to check bounds, not using visited set (causing infinite loops), and incorrect neighbor checks. This feeds into L0 by being the core check within each binary search iteration. Real code pattern: Use a queue with (r,c), visited matrix, directions list, and loop while queue not empty.

examples.trace:
1. Start at (0,0)=1, visited={(0,0)}.
2. Queue: [(0,0)]. Pop (0,0). Check neighbors: (0,1)=2 diff=|1-2|=1≤2 → add (0,1) to queue and visited. (1,0)=3 diff=|1-3|=2≤2 → add (1,0).
3. Queue: [(0,1),(1,0)]. Pop (0,1)=2. Neighbors: (0,0) visited, (0,2)=2 diff=0≤2 → add (0,2), (1,1)=8 diff=6>2 skip.
4. Queue: [(1,0),(0,2)]. Pop (1,0)=3. Neighbors: (0,0) visited, (2,0)=5 diff=2≤2 → add (2,0), (1,1)=8 diff=5>2 skip.
5. Queue: [(0,2),(2,0)]. Pop (0,2)=2. Neighbors: (0,1) visited, (1,2)=2 diff=0≤2 → add (1,2).
6. Queue: [(2,0),(1,2)]. Pop (2,0)=5. Neighbors: (1,0) visited, (2,1)=3 diff=2≤2 → add (2,1).
7. Queue: [(1,2),(2,1)]. Pop (1,2)=2. Neighbors: (0,2) visited, (2,2)=5 diff=3>2 skip.
8. Queue: [(2,1)]. Pop (2,1)=3. Neighbors: (2,0) visited, (2,2)=5 diff=2≤2 → add (2,2).
9. Queue: [(2,2)]. Pop (2,2)=5, which is the target. Return True.

1. Start at (0,0)=1, visited={(0,0)}.
2. Queue: [(0,0)]. Pop (0,0). Check neighbors: (0,1)=2 diff=1>0 skip, (1,0)=3 diff=2>0 skip.
3. Queue empty. Return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search on Monotonic Feasibility
ladders.desc: Use binary search to find the minimum effort value where the BFS feasibility check returns true.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that the feasibility function f(k) — which returns True if a path exists with effort ≤ k — is monotonic: if f(k) is True, then f(k') is True for all k' ≥ k. This monotonicity allows us to apply binary search over the range of possible effort values [0, H], where H is the maximum height difference in the grid (max_height - min_height). We initialize low=0 and high=H. While low < high, compute mid = (low + high) // 2. Check f(mid) using the BFS sub-routine. If True, set high = mid (since we want the minimum, and any value ≥ mid might also be feasible). If False, set low = mid + 1 (since efforts ≤ mid are insufficient). After the loop, low (or high) is the minimum feasible effort. The intuition is similar to finding the first True in a sorted boolean array. Time complexity is O(log H) iterations, each calling BFS O(m*n), so total O(m*n*log H). Space is O(m*n) for BFS. Common mistakes: incorrect initial high (should be H, not a fixed large number), off-by-one in binary search (use while low < high, not ≤), and not handling the case when low==high. This feeds into L0 by providing the search framework that wraps the BFS check from L1. Real code pattern: while low < high: mid = (low+high)//2; if feasible(mid): high = mid; else: low = mid+1; return low.

examples.input: feasibility results: f(0)=F, f(1)=F, f(2)=T, f(3)=T, f(4)=T
examples.trace:
1. Range [0,4] (H=4). low=0, high=4.
2. Mid=(0+4)//2=2. f(2)=True → set high=2.
3. Now low=0, high=2. Mid=1. f(1)=False → set low=2.
4. Now low=2, high=2. Exit loop. Return low=2.

1. Range [0,2]. low=0, high=2.
2. Mid=1. f(1)=True → set high=1.
3. Now low=0, high=1. Mid=0. f(0)=True → set high=0.
4. Now low=0, high=0. Exit. Return 0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Grid Traversal with BFS
ladders.desc: Traverse a 2D grid from start to end using BFS, moving to adjacent cells.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing an m×n grid from a starting cell (e.g., (0,0)) to a target cell (e.g., (m-1,n-1)) using Breadth-First Search (BFS). BFS explores cells level by level, ensuring the shortest path in terms of number of moves (though here we care about connectivity, not shortest distance). We use a queue (e.g., deque) to store cells to visit, and a visited set (e.g., boolean matrix) to avoid revisiting. Start by enqueueing the start cell and marking it visited. While the queue is not empty, dequeue a cell, check if it's the target, and if so, return success. Otherwise, for each of its four neighbors (up, down, left, right), if within bounds and not visited, enqueue the neighbor and mark visited. If the queue empties without reaching the target, return failure. The intuition is that BFS systematically explores all reachable cells, making it ideal for connectivity checks. Time and space are O(m*n). Common mistakes: using DFS which might be less efficient for some grids, forgetting to check bounds, or not marking visited when enqueueing (causing duplicates). This feeds into L1 by forming the base traversal that is enhanced with effort constraints. Real code pattern: directions = [(1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)]; queue = deque([(0,0)]); visited[0][0] = True; while queue: r,c = queue.popleft(); if (r,c) == target: return True; for dr,dc in directions: nr,nc = r+dr, c+dc; if 0<=nr<m and 0<=nc<n and not visited[nr][nc]: visited[nr][nc]=True; queue.append((nr,nc)); return False.

examples.trace:
1. Start at (0,0), visited[0][0]=True, queue=[(0,0)].
2. Pop (0,0). Not target. Neighbors: (0,1) and (1,0) within bounds, not visited → add to queue, mark visited. Queue now [(0,1),(1,0)].
3. Pop (0,1). Not target. Neighbors: (0,0) visited, (0,2) and (1,1) not visited → add, mark. Queue now [(1,0),(0,2),(1,1)].
4. Pop (1,0). Not target. Neighbors: (0,0) visited, (2,0) and (1,1) (1,1 already in queue but not visited? Actually visited when added from step 3) → add (2,0) if not visited. Mark visited. Queue now [(0,2),(1,1),(2,0)].
5. Pop (0,2). Not target. Neighbors: (0,1) visited, (1,2) not visited → add, mark. Queue now [(1,1),(2,0),(1,2)].
6. Pop (1,1). Not target. Neighbors: (0,1) visited, (2,1) not visited → add, mark. Queue now [(2,0),(1,2),(2,1)].
7. Pop (2,0). Not target. Neighbors: (1,0) visited, (2,1) already in queue. Queue now [(1,2),(2,1)].
8. Pop (1,2). Not target. Neighbors: (0,2) visited, (2,2) not visited → add, mark. Queue now [(2,1),(2,2)].
9. Pop (2,1). Not target. Neighbors: (1,1) visited, (2,0) visited, (2,2) already in queue. Queue now [(2,2)].
10. Pop (2,2). This is the target. Return True.

1. Start (0,0), visited[0][0]=True, queue=[(0,0)].
2. Pop (0,0). Not target. Neighbors: (0,1) and (1,0) → add, mark. Queue=[(0,1),(1,0)].
3. Pop (0,1). Not target. Neighbors: (0,0) visited, (1,1) → add, mark. Queue=[(1,0),(1,1)].
4. Pop (1,0). Not target. Neighbors: (0,0) visited, (1,1) already in queue. Queue=[(1,1)].
5. Pop (1,1). Target. Return True.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check Adjacent Cell Validity in Grid
ladders.desc: Given a cell in a 2D grid, check if moving to a neighboring cell is valid based on bounds and conditions.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves checking whether moving from a current cell (r,c) to a neighbor cell (nr,nc) in a grid is valid. The check has two parts: 1) Bounds check: ensure nr and nc are within the grid dimensions (0 ≤ nr < m and 0 ≤ nc < n). 2) Condition check: based on the problem, such as checking if the height difference between grid[r][c] and grid[nr][nc] is ≤ a given effort k. For this sub-problem, we focus on the bounds check and a generic condition (e.g., always true for simple traversal). The intuition is that grid traversal requires validating each move to avoid index errors and apply problem-specific constraints. This operation is performed for each of the four directions (up, down, left, right). Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in bounds (e.g., using ≤ instead of <), forgetting to check both row and column bounds, and not handling edge cells properly. This feeds into L3 by being the core step in BFS when exploring neighbors. Real code pattern: directions = [(1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)]; for dr,dc in directions: nr, nc = r+dr, c+dc; if 0 <= nr < m and 0 <= nc < n and condition(grid[r][c], grid[nr][nc]): # proceed.

examples.input: grid = [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]], current=(0,0), direction=(0,1) (right), condition: always true
examples.trace:
1. Current cell (0,0). Neighbor (0,1) by adding direction (0,1).
2. Check bounds: nr=0, nc=1. Grid has m=2 rows, n=3 columns. 0≤0<2 True, 0≤1<3 True.
3. Condition is always true.
4. Move is valid.

examples.input: grid = [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]], current=(1,2), direction=(1,0) (down), condition: always true
examples.trace:
1. Current (1,2). Neighbor (2,2) by adding (1,0).
2. Check bounds: nr=2, nc=2. m=2, so 0≤2<2 is False (2 is not less than 2).
3. Move invalid due to out of bounds.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Absolute Difference and Comparison
ladders.desc: Compute the absolute difference between two numbers and compare it to a threshold.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: given two integers a and b, compute their absolute difference |a - b|, and check if this difference is less than or equal to a threshold value k. The absolute difference is the positive distance between the numbers, calculated as a-b if a ≥ b, or b-a if b > a. In programming, use abs(a-b). Then, compare abs(a-b) ≤ k. If true, the difference is within the allowed effort; if false, it exceeds the effort. The intuition is that in the path effort problem, moving between cells requires the height difference to be within a limit to ensure the path is feasible. This operation is fundamental to the condition check in L4. Common mistakes: forgetting to use abs (leading to negative differences), incorrect comparison operators (e.g., using < instead of ≤), and not understanding that effort is defined as the maximum difference along the path. This feeds into L4 by providing the specific condition for grid traversal. Real code pattern: if abs(grid[r][c] - grid[nr][nc]) <= k: # move is allowed.

examples.trace:
1. Compute absolute difference: |5-3| = 2.
2. Compare to k=2: 2 ≤ 2 is True.
3. So difference is within threshold.

1. Absolute difference: |10-1| = 9.
2. Compare to k=5: 9 ≤ 5 is False.
3. Difference exceeds threshold.

approaches.name: Dijkstra's Algorithm O(m*n*log(m*n))
approaches.summary: Treat the grid as a graph where nodes are cells and edge weights are the absolute differences in heights between adjacent cells. Use Dijkstra's algorithm with a priority queue to find the path from start to end that minimizes the maximum edge weight along the path. This directly computes the minimum effort without guessing.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Effort Path with Dijkstra's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding path from top-left to bottom-right that minimizes maximum absolute height difference between adjacent cells.
ladders.explanation: This problem asks us to find a path through a grid where the 'effort' is defined as the maximum absolute height difference between any two adjacent cells along the path. We need to minimize this maximum effort. The key insight is that we can treat this as a shortest path problem in a graph where nodes are grid cells and edge weights are the absolute differences between adjacent cell heights. However, instead of minimizing the sum of edge weights (traditional Dijkstra), we need to minimize the maximum edge weight along any path. This is a minimax path problem. We can adapt Dijkstra's algorithm by modifying the relaxation step: when considering moving from cell A to cell B, the 'cost' to reach B is the maximum of (1) the current maximum effort to reach A, and (2) the absolute difference between heights of A and B. We use a priority queue (min-heap) to always expand the node with the smallest current maximum effort. The algorithm starts at (0,0) with effort 0, processes neighbors, updates efforts if we find a smaller maximum effort path, and continues until we reach (m-1,n-1). The time complexity is O(m*n*log(m*n)) because each cell can be pushed/popped from the priority queue, and space is O(m*n) for the distance array and priority queue. Common mistakes include using traditional Dijkstra that sums weights instead of tracking maximums, not handling the priority queue correctly (forgetting to push updated efforts), or using BFS which doesn't guarantee optimality for this minimax problem. The connection to L1 is that this full solution implements the complete Dijkstra adaptation with the modified relaxation logic.

examples.trace: Initialize: efforts array with INF except start (0,0)=0. PQ: [(0,0,0)]. Process (0,0): height=1. Check neighbor (0,1): height=2, diff=|1-2|=1, new_effort=max(0,1)=1 < INF, update efforts[0][1]=1, push (1,0,1). Check (1,0): height=3, diff=2, new_effort=2 < INF, update efforts[1][0]=2, push (2,1,0). PQ now: [(1,0,1),(2,1,0)]. Process (0,1): height=2. Check (0,2): height=2, diff=0, new_effort=max(1,0)=1 < INF, update efforts[0][2]=1, push (1,0,2). Check (1,1): height=8, diff=6, new_effort=6 < INF, update efforts[1][1]=6, push (6,1,1). PQ: [(1,0,2),(2,1,0),(6,1,1)]. Process (0,2): height=2. Check (1,2): height=2, diff=0, new_effort=max(1,0)=1 < INF, update efforts[1][2]=1, push (1,1,2). PQ: [(1,1,2),(2,1,0),(6,1,1)]. Process (1,2): height=2. Check (2,2): height=5, diff=3, new_effort=max(1,3)=3 < INF, update efforts[2][2]=3, push (3,2,2). PQ: [(2,1,0),(3,2,2),(6,1,1)]. Process (1,0): height=3. Check (2,0): height=5, diff=2, new_effort=max(2,2)=2 < INF, update efforts[2][0]=2, push (2,2,0). Check (1,1): height=8, diff=5, new_effort=max(2,5)=5 < 6, update efforts[1][1]=5, push (5,1,1). PQ: [(2,2,0),(2,2,2),(3,2,2),(5,1,1),(6,1,1)]. Process (2,0): height=5. Check (2,1): height=3, diff=2, new_effort=max(2,2)=2 < INF, update efforts[2][1]=2, push (2,2,1). PQ: [(2,2,1),(2,2,2),(3,2,2),(5,1,1),(6,1,1)]. Process (2,1): height=3. Check (2,2): height=5, diff=2, new_effort=max(2,2)=2 < 3, update efforts[2][2]=2, push (2,2,2). Now efforts[2][2]=2. Continue processing but target reached with effort 2. Return 2.

Initialize: efforts all INF except (0,0)=0. PQ: [(0,0,0)]. Process (0,0): height=1. Neighbors: (0,1): diff=1, new_effort=1, update. (1,0): diff=2, new_effort=2, update. PQ: [(1,0,1),(2,1,0)]. Process (0,1): height=2. (0,2): diff=1, new_effort=max(1,1)=1, update efforts[0][2]=1. (1,1): diff=6, new_effort=6, update. PQ: [(1,0,2),(2,1,0),(6,1,1)]. Process (0,2): height=3. (1,2): diff=1, new_effort=max(1,1)=1, update efforts[1][2]=1. PQ: [(1,1,2),(2,1,0),(6,1,1)]. Process (1,2): height=4. (2,2): diff=1, new_effort=max(1,1)=1, update efforts[2][2]=1. Target reached with effort 1. Return 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Modified Dijkstra Relaxation for Minimax Path
ladders.desc: Implementing the core relaxation step that updates maximum effort when moving between cells.
ladders.explanation: In traditional Dijkstra's algorithm for shortest paths, when we move from node u to node v with edge weight w, we update the distance to v if distance[u] + w < distance[v]. However, for our minimax path problem where we want to minimize the maximum edge weight along the path, we need a different relaxation rule. The key insight is that the effort to reach cell v via cell u is the maximum of two values: (1) the current maximum effort to reach u (which represents the worst height difference encountered so far on that path), and (2) the absolute height difference between cells u and v (which is the potential new maximum if this edge has a larger difference). So new_effort = max(effort[u], abs(height[u] - height[v])). We update effort[v] if new_effort < effort[v]. This relaxation ensures we always track the minimum possible maximum edge weight to reach each cell. The algorithm maintains a priority queue (min-heap) where we process cells in order of their current minimum maximum effort. Each time we pop a cell, we examine its four neighbors (up, down, left, right), compute new_effort using the formula above, and if it improves the neighbor's effort, we update it and push the neighbor to the priority queue. This sub-routine is the heart of the full solution. Time complexity for processing one cell is O(1) for the relaxation checks (constant 4 neighbors), but overall it integrates into the O(m*n*log(m*n)) algorithm. Space is O(1) per relaxation. Common mistakes include using sum instead of max, not comparing with current effort correctly, or forgetting to push updated nodes to the priority queue. The connection to L0 is that this relaxation logic is embedded in the main Dijkstra loop. The connection to L2 is that this implements the core minimax insight.

examples.input: Current cell u at (0,0) with height=1, effort[u]=0. Neighbor v at (0,1) with height=3. Current effort[v]=INF.
examples.trace: Compute absolute difference: abs(1-3)=2. Compute new_effort = max(effort[u]=0, diff=2) = 2. Compare new_effort=2 with current effort[v]=INF. Since 2 < INF, update effort[v]=2. Push (2,0,1) to priority queue.

examples.input: Current cell u at (1,1) with height=5, effort[u]=3. Neighbor v at (1,2) with height=4. Current effort[v]=4.
examples.trace: Compute absolute difference: abs(5-4)=1. Compute new_effort = max(effort[u]=3, diff=1) = 3. Compare new_effort=3 with current effort[v]=4. Since 3 < 4, we would normally update, but wait: Actually 3 < 4, so we SHOULD update effort[v] to 3. Let me correct: new_effort=3, current effort[v]=4, 3 < 4, so update effort[v]=3 and push to PQ. If current effort[v] was 2, then 3 > 2, so no update.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Minimax Path Finding in Weighted Graphs
ladders.desc: Understanding how to find a path that minimizes the maximum edge weight rather than the sum.
ladders.explanation: The core logic shift from traditional shortest path problems is that we're not trying to minimize the sum of edge weights (like in classic Dijkstra), but rather minimize the maximum single edge weight encountered along any path. This is called a minimax or bottleneck path problem. Intuition: Think of the height differences as 'obstacles'—we want to find a path where the largest obstacle is as small as possible. The key insight is that we can transform this problem into a standard shortest path problem by redefining what 'distance' means. Instead of distance being cumulative sum, we define distance to a node as the minimum possible maximum edge weight along any path to that node. This allows us to use Dijkstra's algorithm with a modified relaxation rule: when considering an edge from u to v with weight w, the candidate distance to v is max(distance[u], w). We take the minimum of all such candidates. Why does this work? Because if we have a path to u with maximum edge weight M, and we add an edge with weight w, the new path's maximum edge weight is max(M, w). We want the smallest possible such maximum. Dijkstra's algorithm greedily expands nodes with the smallest current maximum, which guarantees optimality because any alternative path to a node would have to have a maximum edge weight at least as large. This is similar to finding the minimum spanning tree's maximum edge weight between two nodes, but here we're finding it in a general graph. The time complexity for the concept is O(E log V) with Dijkstra. Common mistakes include confusing with minimizing sum, or trying to use BFS which doesn't handle weights optimally. The connection to L1 is that this logic is implemented in the relaxation step. The connection to L3 is that this builds on understanding graph traversal with priority queues.

examples.input: Graph with nodes A,B,C. Edges: A-B weight 5, A-C weight 3, C-B weight 2. Find minimax path from A to B.
examples.trace: Path A-B directly: max edge weight = 5. Path A-C-B: max(max(A-C=3), max(C-B=2)) = max(3,2)=3. Since 3 < 5, minimax path is A-C-B with maximum weight 3.

examples.input: Graph: A-B=1, B-C=4, A-D=2, D-C=3. Find minimax A to C.
examples.trace: Path A-B-C: max(1,4)=4. Path A-D-C: max(2,3)=3. Minimax is 3 via A-D-C.

ladders.title: Building Block — Dijkstra's Algorithm with Priority Queue
ladders.desc: Implementing standard Dijkstra's algorithm for shortest paths in a weighted graph.
ladders.explanation: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest paths from a source node to all other nodes in a graph with non-negative edge weights. The intuition is greedy: always expand the node with the smallest known distance first, because any other path to that node would be longer. The algorithm maintains a distance array where distance[v] is the current shortest known distance from source to v, initialized to infinity except source=0. It uses a priority queue (min-heap) to process nodes in order of increasing distance. Steps: 1. Push (0, source) to PQ. 2. While PQ not empty: pop node u with smallest distance. 3. For each neighbor v of u with edge weight w: if distance[u] + w < distance[v], update distance[v] = distance[u] + w and push (distance[v], v) to PQ. 4. Repeat until PQ empty. The algorithm guarantees that when a node is popped from PQ, its distance is finalized (optimal). Time complexity is O((V+E) log V) with binary heap, as each node and edge can be processed. Space is O(V) for distances and PQ. Common mistakes include not using a priority queue (using regular queue leads to incorrect results for weighted graphs), not marking nodes as visited (though with PQ, once popped, distance is final), or handling negative weights (Dijkstra doesn't work with negative weights). The connection to L2 is that this standard algorithm is adapted for minimax by changing the relaxation rule. The connection to L4 is that this uses priority queues as a fundamental data structure.

examples.input: Graph: nodes 0,1,2; edges: 0-1 weight 2, 0-2 weight 5, 1-2 weight 1. Source=0.
examples.trace: Initialize distances: [0, INF, INF]. PQ: [(0,0)]. Pop (0,0). Neighbors: to 1: 0+2=2 < INF, update dist[1]=2, push (2,1). to 2: 0+5=5 < INF, update dist[2]=5, push (5,2). PQ: [(2,1),(5,2)]. Pop (2,1). Neighbor 2: 2+1=3 < 5, update dist[2]=3, push (3,2). PQ: [(3,2),(5,2)]. Pop (3,2). No update. Final distances: [0,2,3].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Priority Queue (Min-Heap) for Graph Traversal
ladders.desc: Using a min-heap to always process the node with smallest current distance in Dijkstra's algorithm.
ladders.explanation: A priority queue, specifically a min-heap, is a data structure that allows efficient insertion and extraction of the minimum element. In Dijkstra's algorithm, we need to repeatedly find and remove the node with the smallest current distance estimate. Using a min-heap gives O(log n) time for both push and pop operations, making the overall algorithm efficient. Intuition: By always processing the node with the smallest known distance first, we guarantee that once we pop a node, we've found the shortest path to it (given non-negative weights). This greedy approach works because any alternative path to that node would have to go through a node with larger distance, making it longer. Implementation: We store pairs (distance, node) in the heap. When we update a node's distance, we push the new (distance, node) into the heap. Note that we might have multiple entries for the same node in the heap with different distances; we ignore outdated ones by checking if the popped distance matches the current distance in our distance array. Common operations: heap.push((dist, node)), heap.pop() returns smallest, heapify to build heap. Time complexity: O(log n) per push/pop. Space: O(n). Common mistakes include using a regular queue (FIFO) which doesn't prioritize by distance, not handling duplicate entries correctly, or using max-heap by accident. The connection to L3 is that this priority queue is essential for Dijkstra's efficiency. The connection to L5 is that this builds on basic queue concepts.

examples.input: Initial heap: [(0,A)]. After processing A, push neighbors: B with dist 2, C with dist 5.
examples.trace: Start with heap containing (0,A). Pop (0,A) - process A. Discover B with distance 2: push (2,B) to heap. Discover C with distance 5: push (5,C) to heap. Heap now has (2,B) and (5,C); min is (2,B). Next pop will be (2,B).

examples.input: Heap: [(3,X),(5,Y),(7,Z)]. Pop, then push new entry for Y with updated dist 4.
examples.trace: Current heap: [(3,X),(5,Y),(7,Z)]. Pop min: (3,X) removed. Process X, update Y's distance from 5 to 4. Push (4,Y) to heap. Now heap has (4,Y),(5,Y),(7,Z). Note duplicate for Y; when we pop (4,Y), we'll use it and ignore (5,Y) later if popped.

testCases.expected: First pop (2,Y), second pop (4,W), third pop (5,Z)

testCases.input: Heap: [(0,S)]; Pop; push (2,T), (1,U); Pop; push (3,V); Pop
testCases.expected: First pop (0,S), second pop (1,U), third pop (2,T)
First pop (0,3), second pop (3,2), third pop (6,1)
First pop (4,A) (or B, tie), second pop (2,C)
First pop (5,Y), second pop (6,W), third pop (7,X)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation and Adjacency
ladders.desc: Representing a grid as a graph where cells are nodes and adjacent cells are connected by edges.
ladders.explanation: A graph consists of nodes (vertices) and edges connecting them. For a 2D grid, we can model each cell as a node. Two cells are adjacent if they share a side (up, down, left, right—not diagonally). This creates an implicit graph where edges exist between neighboring cells. The 'weight' of an edge can be defined based on the problem; here, it's the absolute difference in heights between the two cells. Intuition: This representation allows us to use graph algorithms like Dijkstra to solve grid-based pathfinding problems. We don't need to explicitly build an adjacency list; we can generate neighbors on the fly using the grid indices. For a cell at (i,j), its neighbors are (i-1,j), (i+1,j), (i,j-1), (i,j+1), provided they are within grid bounds (0 <= i < m, 0 <= j < n). This is a common pattern in grid problems. Time complexity to get neighbors is O(1) per cell. Space is O(1) if we generate dynamically. Common mistakes include including diagonal neighbors, going out of bounds, or confusing row/column indices. The connection to L4 is that this graph is traversed using priority queues in Dijkstra. This foundation is essential for mapping the physical grid to an abstract graph structure.

examples.trace: Cell (1,1): Check up: (1-1,1) = (0,1) within bounds (rows 0-2, cols 0-2). Check down: (1+1,1)=(2,1) within bounds. Check left: (1,1-1)=(1,0) within bounds. Check right: (1,1+1)=(1,2) within bounds. All four are valid neighbors.
Cell (0,0): Up: (0-1,0)=(-1,0) out of bounds (row <0). Down: (0+1,0)=(1,0) valid. Left: (0,0-1)=(0,-1) out of bounds. Right: (0,0+1)=(0,1) valid. So neighbors: (1,0) and (0,1).
